text.title: JTBD Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules every JTBD application must enforce. These are the HARD rules — user-supplied input must not override them.

rule.statement: Define the job at a level of abstraction that stays stable over decades. Solutions change, jobs do not.
rule.rationale: Defining the job as "use Notion" locks the product to a solution. "Capture and organize thoughts" reveals competition across paper, typewriters, Word, Notion — and what comes next.

rule.statement: Capture functional, emotional, AND social dimensions for every job. Never report only the functional dimension.
rule.rationale: Emotional and social dimensions explain price tolerance and churn. A customer who leaves despite the product "working fine" is usually leaving over an emotional or social job failure.

rule.statement: Tag each interview statement with Push / Pull / Habit / Fear and assign severity 1-5. Always include all four forces — never only Push and Pull.
rule.rationale: Adoption = (Push + Pull) - (Habit + Fear). Founders over-focus on Pull and ignore Habit and Fear. For niche B2B, inertia and switching anxiety often dominate and are the actual blockers.

rule.statement: Interview only people who switched (to, away from, or first adopted the category) within the last 60-90 days.

rationale.source: memory-decay studies cited in JTBD literature

rule.rationale: Recent memory provides specific trigger events, exact friction sources, and authentic emotional context. After 90 days switchers reconstruct a rationalized narrative, not the decision process.

rule.statement: Synthesize job statements only after tagging ≥5 interviews. Reject any candidate statement not backed by ≥3 verbatim quotes from real switchers.
rule.rationale: LLMs generate plausible job statements without interview data. Verbatim quote requirement makes the statement falsifiable.

example.title: Stable job vs solution
example.code:
Stable job: "Communicate with distant family" Solutions: letters → phone → email → video calls
Stable job: "Capture and organize thoughts" Solutions: paper → typewriter → Word → Notion
example.note: Illustrates r1: the job-level abstraction reveals competitive replacement across decades.

example.title: Job defined at solution-level
example.code: Stated job: "use Notion for note-taking" Problem: this is a solution, not a job. Roadmap will defend Notion-shaped features rather than reveal what comes next. Repair: re-state at job level — "capture and organize thoughts so I can find them later when I need them."
example.note: Illustrates r1 violation — a frequent LLM failure mode where it accepts the founder's product framing as the job.
